description: This figure presents a scatter chart plotting the index of hydrogen embrittlement (IHE) versus ultimate tensile strength (UTS) for several austenitic steels and multicomponent face-centered-cubic alloys tested under gaseous hydrogen charging conditions. The horizontal axis displays IHE from 0.7 to -0.2, the vertical axis shows UTS in MPa from 400 to 1100, and a top color gradient bar visually ranks HE resistance from low (red) to high (blue). The caption reports that the plot is divided into three threshold regions: low resistance (IHE > 0.1), moderate (0.1 > IHE > 0), and high (IHE < 0). The legend identifies four fracture surface characteristics: small-sized dimple/dimple fracture (SD/D, blue), ductile and intergranular fracture (D+IG, light blue), hydrogen-induced successive slip or quasi-cleavage (HIS, orange), and intergranular/transgranular plus quasi-cleavage (IG/TG + QC, red). Alloys with low HE resistance, such as FeMnC TWIP steels and 304/316 stainless steels, exhibit high positive IHE values (~0.15 to 0.6) and are linked to brittle IG/TG+QC or HIS modes. By contrast, high-resistance materials—including CoNiV, HP160, 310S, CoCrFeMnNi, FeNiCrCo, 316L, and 317L—cluster at IHE ≤ 0 within an elliptical highlight and predominantly show SD/D or D+IG fracture. The present Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel is marked by a distinctive starburst symbol at approximately IHE = -0.05 and UTS ≈ 585 MPa, placing it in the high-resistance region. The caption states that this positioning, obtained with a hydrogen content of ~101 mass ppm, confirms the present steel's exceptional HE resistance comparable to top-performing alloys, attributing it to hydrogen-enhanced microbanding and related plasticity accommodation mechanisms.
